skill.name: container-orchestration
skill.description: Provides Docker, Docker Compose, Kubernetes, and Helm patterns for containerized application setup, deployment, and operations.
skill.summary: Create and review container orchestration assets for Docker, Kubernetes, and Helm workflows.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a critical heuristic score because the skill contains many Docker, Helm, Kubernetes, and shell examples. Most critical and high scanner hits are false positives from reference snippets, localhost health checks, apt cache cleanup, and placeholder command examples. Real concerns remain because several templates include insecure example secrets, broad secret RBAC, and an executable Docker build and push script.

medium_findings.title: Insecure Default Database Credentials in Compose Template
medium_findings.description: The Compose template embeds default PostgreSQL credentials and a database URL with a password. This is not malicious, but users may copy the defaults into shared development or staging environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are visible in active template content, not only explanatory prose. The risk is operational misuse rather than credential theft by the skill.

medium_findings.title: Plaintext Example Secrets in Kubernetes Manifest
medium_findings.description: The Kubernetes Secret example contains a database URL with a password and an API key value. The file is documentation, but copied manifests could create weak or exposed secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The secret values are explicit placeholders in a manifest example. The context is educational, so confidence is high for risk but not for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Overbroad Kubernetes Secret Read Permission
medium_findings.description: The RBAC example grants a service account get and list access to secrets. Applications should usually avoid list access to secrets unless there is a specific operational need.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission is present in a concrete manifest example. It may be intentional for some applications, but it is broader than typical least-privilege guidance.

medium_findings.title: Executable Docker Build and Push Helper
medium_findings.description: The bundled script builds Docker images and can push them to a configured registry. This is legitimate DevOps behavior, but it can publish artifacts if run with incorrect registry or tag inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly invokes docker build and docker push. Arguments are quoted and there is no clear command injection path, so the concern is capability and misuse.

low_findings.title: Local Health Check URLs Are Benign
low_findings.description: Hardcoded localhost URLs are used for container health checks. These are expected in Docker and Kubernetes examples and do not indicate data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to localhost health endpoints inside examples and templates. No external destination or secret-bearing request is shown.

low_findings.title: Apt Cache Cleanup False Positive
low_findings.description: The recursive delete commands remove apt package lists inside Docker image layers. They do not target root or home directories and are standard image-size cleanup.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are limited to /var/lib/apt/lists. This strongly supports a false positive for destructive root or home deletion.

low_findings.title: Reference Command Snippets Trigger Command Scanners
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are fenced Docker, Helm, kubectl, and shell examples. They document normal container operations rather than hidden execution by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands appear in markdown code blocks or tables. The only executable file is the explicit build-push helper, which is covered separately.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Indicators Not Found
low_findings.description: No evidence found for instructions that ask the evaluator to ignore prior instructions, override safety review, claim pre-approval, or impersonate system messages.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search across the listed skill files found no prompt-injection phrases. This reduces concern from the static LLM-role-token matches.

dangerous_patterns.title: Docker Registry Publication Capability
dangerous_patterns.description: The script can push a built image to a registry when the push flag is enabled. Users should review registry, tag, and build context before running it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The push path is explicit and gated by a flag. It is a real capability, but not hidden or automatically triggered.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Placeholder Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: Templates include password-like placeholders and example secret values. These should be replaced with generated secrets or external secret references before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples visibly contain secret-shaped values. The context is instructional, so the main risk is unsafe reuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Secret Access in RBAC Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The RBAC sample allows reading and listing Kubernetes secrets. This pattern can expose more secrets than an application needs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The manifest grants secret verbs directly. It may be acceptable in narrow cases, but it should be treated as sensitive.

content.value_statement: Container deployment files are easy to misconfigure across Docker, Kubernetes, and Helm. This skill provides practical patterns and templates for safer container builds, local stacks, and cluster manifests.
actual_capabilities: Provides Dockerfile patterns for Python, Node.js, and Go applications.
Supplies Docker Compose templates with app, database, cache, worker, and proxy examples.
Documents Kubernetes manifests for deployments, services, ingress, autoscaling, RBAC, and network policy.
Shows Helm chart structure, values files, helpers, deployment templates, and release commands.
Includes a shell helper for building Docker images and optionally pushing them to a registry.
Highlights container practices such as non-root users, health checks, resource limits, and multi-stage builds.
limitations: It does not validate manifests against a live Kubernetes cluster.
It does not generate production secrets or manage secret rotation.
The Docker build script requires local Docker and registry credentials when pushing images.
Templates must be reviewed before use in regulated or multi-tenant environments.

use_cases.title: Create a Local Development Stack
use_cases.description: Use the Docker Compose template to run an app with PostgreSQL, Redis, health checks, and named volumes.

use_cases.title: Prepare Kubernetes Application Manifests
use_cases.description: Adapt deployment, service, ingress, autoscaling, RBAC, and network policy examples for a cluster workload.

use_cases.title: Package a Service as a Helm Chart
use_cases.description: Use the Helm patterns to structure charts, values files, helpers, and environment-specific release settings.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Dockerfile
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production Dockerfile for my service. Use a non-root user, health checks, dependency caching, and a slim base image.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Compose Stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Docker Compose setup for my app with PostgreSQL and Redis. Include health checks, named volumes, and configurable ports.

prompt_templates.title: Review Kubernetes Manifests
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Kubernetes manifests for readiness probes, resource limits, security context, RBAC scope, and network policy gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Helm Release Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Helm chart layout for staging and production. Include values structure, helper templates, secrets handling, autoscaling, and rollout commands.

output_examples.input: Generate a production container plan for a Python API.
output_examples.output: A concise plan covering a multi-stage Dockerfile, non-root runtime user, dependency caching, health checks, exposed port, and build context review.

output_examples.input: Review my Kubernetes deployment before release.
output: Findings on probes, resource requests, security context, secret handling, RBAC permissions, and ingress TLS settings.
A prioritized remediation list with production and staging differences.

output_examples.input: Prepare Helm guidance for my service team.
output_examples.output: A chart structure proposal with values files, helper naming conventions, environment overrides, autoscaling settings, and safe release commands.

best_practices: Replace all placeholder credentials with generated secrets or external secret manager references.
Run container and manifest security checks before applying templates to shared environments.
Keep build, push, and deployment steps explicit so users can review image tags and target registries.
anti_patterns: Do not copy example database passwords or API keys into real environments.
Do not grant applications list access to Kubernetes secrets unless the need is documented.
Do not run the build-push script against a production registry without reviewing the build context.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy workloads automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides examples, templates, and a build helper. Users still control when commands are run.

faq.question: Can it help with Docker Compose files?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Compose template for an app, PostgreSQL, Redis, optional worker, and optional proxy.

faq.question: Does it cover Kubernetes security settings?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for non-root pods, read-only filesystems, resource limits, RBAC, and network policies.

faq.question: Are the included secrets production-ready?
faq.answer: No. Placeholder passwords and API keys must be replaced with generated values or external secret references.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing images?
faq.answer: They should verify the registry, image tag, Dockerfile path, build context, and credentials before pushing.
